item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be sourced, and thus is assumed to be non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). As such, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Anyone feel like this should be euclid if not keter, what with the information -a has?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's…interesting

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Should be noted that this article used to be SCP-1750, but Esko has given A Random Day to post an article in this slot, moving the old 1750 to this slot instead.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Alrighty. So this crosslink suggestions actually comes from the mind of DrBleep. We feel that given the clear power and dangerous potential for SCP-1750-A to become a threat to the Foundation via its resource base and knowledge of the Foundation's resources, the SCPF would pull out all the stops in trying to locate SCP-1750-A, including attempting to use SCP-2232 to track her location. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

Once payment is complete, individuals separate from SCP-1750 and the iteration rapidly reduces in size to normal. SCP-1750-A has proved cooperative in containment and removal of SCP-1750 instances, so long as payment is forthcoming. SCP-1750-A has shown an extensive and unknown knowledge of Foundation locations, protocols, and other sensitive information.

To this:

Once payment is complete, individuals separate from SCP-1750 and the iteration rapidly reduces in size to normal. SCP-1750-A has proved cooperative in containment and removal of SCP-1750 instances, so long as payment is forthcoming. SCP-1750-A has shown an extensive and unknown knowledge of Foundation locations, protocols, and other sensitive information1.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. Attempts to track SCP-1750-A via SCP-2232 and similar anomalies are ongoing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll volunteer to PM Eskobar.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's been like three and a half years and I'm still hungry for the answers to these questions

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When not being touched, SCP-1750 remains in a fixed position in whatever location it last inhabited while being touched. SCP-1750 is immovable except during human contact.

Can it be moved by a human who is wearing gloves, or does it only move when in contact with human skin?

When a human is in contact with SCP-1750, the object will become mobile; however, it will also begin perpetually growing in size.

If the subject's arm is cut off, or if the subject is killed, does it stop growing?

Additionally, SCP-1750 will adhere to any exposed human skin; no means of separating skin from SCP-1750 has yet been determined.

1) If enough force is exerted on the ball, can the skin be ripped loose from their flesh, in order to get free from the ball?

2) Does it adhere to anything besides skin? Like, what if someone licked it, would it stick to their tongue?

Between five and fifteen minutes of SCP-1750 coming into contact with a human, the nearest cellular telephone to SCP-1750 will ring; the individual on the other end of the call will request to be put on the line with the individual holding SCP-1750.

What if the subject is incapable of talking?

Should an affected individual fail to have any acceptable form of payment, they are offered to complete an unspecified form of labor for the benefit of SCP-1750-A.

What if the subject refuses to comply after agreeing?

First recorded transcript of SCP-1750-A contact. Affected individual identified as David Chambliss, 17 years old, convenience store clerk living in Clarksburg, TN.

How did the Foundation manage to get a recording of this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Technetium is almost entirely composed of Tc99 which is a weak beta emitter. These are not powerful enough to penetrate skin, so the only significant danger would be from inhalation of Tc particles. In the lab, a fume hood is sufficent to protect against this, and in the field, I guess a face mask would work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, that's valid. Edited.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a minor point, but…

+ show block
– hide block

Once in contact with the individual in contact with SCP-1750

… I find the double use of the word "contact" here to be a bit awkward. Maybe a slight change like,

Once in contact with the individual affected by SCP-1750

or

Once allowed to speak with the individual touching SCP-1750

would be better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Spheres in a cubic containment cell. -2.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gosh, I love this a lot and I'm not sure why exactly. +1

item.title: Re: Rewrite, ish
item.content:encoded: I kind of miss the marshmallows, but oil and technetium are a decent replacement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like to think that it is an innocent mistake on the alien's part. They say that on-site removal is free, but since you are so far away you have to pay, and since you are so far away, they have no way to retrieve the ball. I totally understand the Alien Con-men part and I am sure that is what it was written in mind with, but I like to think that not everybody is a money grubbing con-artists, even if they are. Now I have to go answer this email from a Nigerian prince wanting to store money in my bank account. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is not anomalous capitalism, it's extortion. LOLOL SAME DIFFERENCE UNF UNF PUBLIC EDUCASHUNS

I love it either way, I find myself fond of SCPs that involve weird deals conducted with faceless people over the phone. It feels like we have a lot of such things, but I have no complaints about that. It feels like fertile ground for horror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read through this entire comment section just to see what could have possibly made you post that, Eskobar. My goodness what I miss out on when I decide to veg for a long holiday weekend!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: U A BITHC

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spheres. -1.

Troll post

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes because how dare an article be whimsical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know what? Maybe the problem is NOT ENOUGH WHIMSY.

People are complaining that it seems like a lead up to a joke. The problem isn't the joke, it's the lack of a punchline! Maybe. Well, it's worth thinking about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel this is less a mistake than it is some type of protection racket. Which would explain the oh-so-inconvenient static over the company name.

Read the other comments, it is a con! Alien conmen! Yeah! I understood the concept!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Precisely why the company would want other people to handle them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Note that technetium is radioactive. Relatively mildly so, but it's a significant component of nuclear waste. Handling technetium nuts and bolts in the manner described wouldn't be instant death, more on the level of a serious cancer risk.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't help but read a run-on sentence, even if I am reading it in Janine's voice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Going to run through this when I'm less sleepy. The only thing I can definitively say right now is that the conversation logs are punctuated that way to emphasize the way the speaker is talking—very clipped, lots of run-on sentences. I promise I know what's grammatically correct, but for a transcript, some of the point *is* conveying tone of voice. Nobody speaks with semicolons:)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The general phrasing could use… simplification? clarification? Something. The tone bothers me. Some examples of what I mean, but there are plenty more:

  • "60cm^3 cubic containment locker" - is that a 60x60x60, or a 60 cubic cm locker? Confusing especially given the frequent use of volume in the article.
  • "Instances of SCP-1750 should not be permitted to grow beyond 5 cubic meters… Under no circumstances is SCP-1750 to be permitted to continue growth unchecked." This is the same sentence twice, except the second is less informative.
  • "SCP-1750 designates any of eight spiked purple balls" - I think the standard classification procedure here is to say that SCP-1750 is a set of eight objects, designated -1 through -8.
  • "…a Mohs hardness rating of at least 10, at least equivalent to diamond." - at least you said "at least" here.
  • "While not being touched, SCP-1750 remains in a fixed position in whatever location it last inhabited while being touched." Convoluted sentence, poor word choice ("inhabited" for an inanimate object). How about "SCP-1750 is immobile and immovable when not in contact with a human being."
  • The conversation logs are poorly punctuated. Just because it's a transcript doesn't mean it can't be run through a grammar filter for legibility. A corrected version of SCP-1750-A's first line: "Hello Mr. Carter, this is [static] calling from the [static] Corporation. I'm told that you've come into contact with one of our products; is that correct?"
  • Overall, there are just a whole lot of "is unknown," "proven impenetrable," "not possible," etc etc etc. The article is littered with them. It's like every time you state a property of the item, you explain that the Foundation is clueless about it or powerless to deal with it. It just wears on you after a while.

I like the overall idea. I just have a hard time reading it.

EDIT: Cleanup. — Eskobar

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Counterargument: human beings are very, very bad at estimating "inferred" dimensions:
- it's easy to look at a car wheel and say "it's 2ft across," but most people will scoff at the idea that it's over 6ft around (even though "pi*D" is a suuuuper simple equation).
- a bathtub is about 5ft long and 18" deep, but you'll probably surprise someone if you tell them it can contain as much as 100 gallons of water (or that said 100gal weighs nearly half a ton!).

Diameter is a very easy number to parse. I don't know what 525 cubic meters looks like, size-wise. I can very easily imagine a sphere 10m in diameter (and it surprises me to realize that an 8-fold increase in volume is only a doubling of the diameter… 2^3, naturally!).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wasn't sure about this before. Liked it but it was not too engaged with it. But with the addition of the specific labor description and your revamping of the remunerations (hated the marshmallow bit) I feel comfortable with upvoting.

I do miss the bubble gum though.

item.title: Re: Rewrite, ish
item.content:encoded: hhhgngnnhgnhgnhgnng

item.title: Re: Rewrite, ish
item.content:encoded: I bow in your direction, sir.

item.title: Re: Rewrite, ish
item.content:encoded: Removed downvote. Don't like it enough to upvote.

item.title: Re: Rewrite, ish
item.content:encoded: I can do nothing for that. One time when I finished a final paper for a class, I made a mug of hot chocolate with butterscotch schnapps and big-size caramel marshmallows on top. Just take my word for it that you've never had that much nngghhh in one mug. It was sort of the inspiration for that particular item.

item.title: Re: Rewrite, ish
item.content:encoded: I'm still craving caramel marshmallows though. Can you fix that? Like, either grab me a bag of the things or distract me or something.

item.title: Rewrite, ish
item.content:encoded: Okay, I've rewritten the shit out the middle part, or as they call it 'round these parts, "editing". They now only accept standard currency, publicly tradable materials such as oil or gold, and technetium. And a very specific form of labor. Thanks to Reject for banging out the ideas. And Soulless, I also got rid of the bubble-gum part; now the voice part is there just as a descriptor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, gotcha. Will consider revising a bit later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. That incredibly common caricature is sounds like a build-up to somethingfunny or a joke. Aren't super bored secretaries played for vague amusement?

2. That's not what I'm saying. It's the fact that you the author chose to put someone losing his shit in an article, paired with common stereotypical and supposedly amusing caricatures, along with random shit like paying with marshmallows. It, again, sounds like the lead up to some kind of joke or amusing thing. Think about it. Guy freaks out, calls a phone, is losing his shit while calling a a secretary who's just chewing bubblegum obnoxiously and filing her nails, and then ends up paying in something random like marshmallows. It just sounds like a joke.

3. Oh yes. I realize that. But you see, marshmallows and sheep bile come out of complete left field and there's no need to specify random stuff like that over 'This SCP accepts any form of payment even if it isn't considered usual currency' or something, which tells you everything you need to know without throwing the reader for a loop with sheep bile and marshmallows. The fact that this random bile and marshmallows things even is chosen to be written just kind of adds to this 'okay so this is supposed to be played for ridiculous laughs, right?' kind of vibe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yah, I guess so. Still, her company has a pretty good racket going.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but I think I'd like it better if there was an actual implication with what payments the Corporation will accept rather than random shit like caramel marshmallows.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea is that the significant feature is how very, very visible this can become in a hurry. Containment is a cinch, as long as it isn't growing; that's why it's Safe. But when someone's touching it, it can get super visible. Volume seemed like the most important factor in that function. But I'll keep it in mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair enough. A couple of things:

1. SCP-1750-A is supposed to basically sound like Janine Melnitz from Ghostbusters. Whiny, nasal voice, very bored sounding. Just a stereotypical secretary/receptionist type. She really is just a bored worker for a big company, doing a boring job. Stereotype, sure.

2. Let's say you wake up with a tattoo on your forehead with a phone number, you call it, and this same transaction happens. Someone who doesn't give a shit is who you're relying on to end a very weird, inexplicable, somewhat violating situation for you. It's just how I figured I'd react.

3. The payment methods thing is supposed to explain just how much they want their scam to net profit in literally any possible form. They accept *any* payment that the victims can produce in a hurry. And the ball isn't going to wait for, say, a peasant girl in Naples come up with several hundred American dollars. The examples in question are sort of silly, I'll give you that. That I might change if it bothers other people.

But judge as you will.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it would be more useful, in-universe, to know the rate at which it increases in diameter than volume, since it's more important to determine what sort of container is needed to contain the thing at any given point in time than to know how much water it could displace at any given point in time. But yeah, I likes it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There was some whimsy in the original draft, but it's mostly gone now.

NOT THAT THERE'S ANYTHING WRONG WITH WHIMSY

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh yeah. It's neat. I'll give you that. But such a stereotypical caricature of some chick chewing bubblegum, some guy getting overly mad/freaked out about the situation, payment though random-ass mashmallows and sheep bile? It sounds like the build up to some joke to me and there isn't much other than 'well, this concept is neat I guess' thing going for the article and that isn't quite enough for me.

(Maybe I'm just doing it wrong, I'll look at it again later and see if my opinion changes)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kind of got the feeling you were trying to be ~whimsical~ and, whether it was intentional or not, I'm still holding off on voting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I inquire again as to where the funny is. I am absolutely hilarious in real life, so I may have brought the funny when I meant to leave it at home, but it's not intentional, I promise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Where's the lol? Other than the marshmallow thing, and that's more anomalous than lol.

But yeah, it's anomalous capitalism. It's a use of an anomalous item as a con tool, purely for profit. It's the use of the anomalous, the unthinkable, an object that violates the standard rules of physics, to make a quick buck and for no other reason. I didn't see anything really similar to it, so I thought it was neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's a very "lol u guise look at this it's a ball and there's a lady with a phone that calls people lol isn't that quirky? i'm so funny" vibe to this that I don't quite like. No vote, for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I still don't like the 'lol anomalous capitalism'. There's nothing else of interest about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, her company. And the Foundation ain't exactly wanting for cash. MC&D would *kill* for these guys' business model.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is wacky and beautiful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What did it for me was the image of a woman somewhere laughing her head off because every time they test this SCP she gets richer $494.25.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, removed the reference to exponential growth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think there's a name for it. If it were y=v+x5 (an integer exponent), you could call it "polynomial" or "quintic", but I've never heard a name for equations of non-integer exponent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shit, you are correct. Misread the Wikipedia. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's technically cubic growth, but I didn't know if that was an actual term. What would you call the kind of growth described here?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For something to be exponential, the variable needs to be the exponent. It's not enough to just have an exponent. Example: x2 is not exponential, but 2x is exponential.

y=v+x5.04 is not exponential growth.
y=v+5.04x, however, would be exponential growth.

Of course, if you make this change, the object will increase much faster than you've described, so you might want to reduce the value, 5.04.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how the company is taking advantage of people because of its own faults. +1 I might be wrong, but I am pretty sure Moh's Hardness is on a scale of 1-10. Diamond being 10. Other than that, good job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
IT IS COINCIDENCE

I SWEARS IT
